Title:
V-JEPA 2: Self-Supervised Video Models Enable Understanding, Prediction and Planning
Authors:

View PDF

Abstract:
A major challenge for modern AI is to learn to understand the world and learn to act largely by observation. This paper explores a self-supervised approach that combines internet-scale video data with a small amount of interaction data (robot trajectories), to develop models capable of understanding, predicting, and planning in the physical world. We first pre-train an action-free joint-embedding-predictive architecture, V-JEPA 2, on a video and image dataset comprising over 1 million hours of internet video. V-JEPA 2 achieves strong performance on motion understanding (77.3 top-1 accuracy on Something-Something v2) and state-of-the-art performance on human action anticipation (39.7 recall-at-5 on Epic-Kitchens-100) surpassing previous task-specific models. Additionally, after aligning V-JEPA 2 with a large language model, we demonstrate state-of-the-art performance on multiple video question-answering tasks at the 8 billion parameter scale (e.g., 84.0 on PerceptionTest, 76.9 on TempCompass). Finally, we show how self-supervised learning can be applied to robotic planning tasks by post-training a latent action-conditioned world model, V-JEPA 2-AC, using less than 62 hours of unlabeled robot videos from the Droid dataset. We deploy V-JEPA 2-AC zero-shot on Franka arms in two different labs and enable picking and placing of objects using planning with image goals. Notably, this is achieved without collecting any data from the robots in these environments, and without any task-specific training or reward. This work demonstrates how self-supervised learning from web-scale data and a small amount of robot interaction data can yield a world model capable of planning in the physical world.

V-JEPA 2
V-JEPA 2, or Video Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture 2, is a 1.2 billion-parameter self-supervised world model developed by Meta AI for processing and predicting video data, introduced on June 11, 2025, as an advancement in non-generative AI architectures that enable state-of-the-art visual understanding and physical reasoning without relying on explicit pixel generation.
[1]
[2]
Building on the original Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) framework pioneered by Meta's Chief AI Scientist Yann LeCun and his team, V-JEPA 2 trains on internet-scale video datasets combined with limited interaction data, such as robot trajectories, to learn abstract representations of motion, object interactions, and world dynamics.
[3]
[2]
This model excels in applications like zero-shot robot control for tasks including grasping and placing objects, as well as forecasting future video frames to support planning in physical environments, marking a significant step toward scalable, grounded machine intelligence.
[1]
[4]
Key innovations include its joint embedding predictive architecture (JEPA), featuring an encoder and predictor trained in two phases to learn representations of physical world dynamics, outperforming prior self-supervised video models on benchmarks for motion understanding and robotics simulation.
[2]
[5]
Development and Release
Announcement and Release Date
V-JEPA 2 was officially announced by Meta AI on June 11, 2025, marking a significant advancement in self-supervised video modeling. The announcement was detailed in a blog post on the Meta AI website, where the model was introduced as a 1.2 billion-parameter world model trained on internet-scale video data to enable visual understanding, prediction, and planning.
[1]
Simultaneously, the accompanying research paper, titled "V-JEPA 2: Self-Supervised Video Models Enable Understanding, Prediction, and Planning," was published as a preprint on arXiv under the identifier 2506.09985, providing the foundational technical overview.
[2]
As part of the release, Meta made the model openly accessible to the research community, including the release of model weights and PyTorch codebase via GitHub and Hugging Face repositories. This open-source approach facilitated immediate experimentation and benchmarking.
[4]
[6]
[5]
The announcement garnered widespread media attention, with coverage from outlets like PYMNTS and InfoQ. According to Meta, V-JEPA 2 is the first video-trained world model achieving state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance in visual prediction and zero-shot robot control. These reports emphasized its potential to advance machine intelligence by building on the original Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) framework developed by Yann LeCun's team.
[7]
[5]
[1]

The announcement was detailed in a blog post on the Meta AI website, where the model was introduced as a 1.2 billion-parameter world model trained on internet-scale video data to enable visual understanding, prediction, and planning.

According to Meta, V-JEPA 2 is the first video-trained world model achieving state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance in visual prediction and zero-shot robot control. These reports emphasized its potential to advance machine intelligence by building on the original Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) framework developed by Yann LeCun's team.

 31‚ÜíKey Contributors and Affiliations
 32‚ÜíV-JEPA 2 was primarily developed by the Fundamental AI Research (FAIR) team at Meta Platforms, Inc., under the leadership of Yann LeCun, Meta's Chief AI Scientist.
 33‚Üí[3]
 34‚Üí[2]
 35‚ÜíThe project involved a collaborative effort from numerous researchers affiliated with Meta AI, including key contributors such as Mahmoud Assran, Adrien Bardes, David Fan, Quentin Garrido, Ross Howes, Matthew Muckley, Ammar Rizvi, and others.
 36‚Üí[2]
 37‚Üí[4]
 38‚ÜíAdditional contributors included experts from academic partners, such as Jitendra Malik from the University of California, Berkeley, highlighting interdisciplinary collaborations in robotics, computer vision, and machine learning.
 39‚Üí[2]
 40‚ÜíThe development also featured Nicolas Ballas as a key organizer, with the work building on prior JEPA frameworks advanced by LeCun's team at Meta FAIR.
 41‚Üí[2]
 42‚ÜíArchitecture and Training
 43‚ÜíModel Components
 44‚ÜíV-JEPA 2 is a world model featuring a total of 1.2 billion parameters, enabling scalable processing of video data through advanced neural architectures.
 45‚Üí[1]
 46‚ÜíAt its core, the model employs transformer-based encoders to process raw video input and generate joint embeddings that capture semantic information about the observed world, facilitating effective representation of spatio-temporal dynamics.
 47‚Üí[1]
 48‚ÜíBuilding on the Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) framework, V-JEPA 2 incorporates a predictor network that receives these video embeddings along with contextual inputs to forecast future embeddings, adapted specifically for video through integrated spatio-temporal processing to handle motion and temporal sequences.
 49‚Üí[1]
 50‚Üí[8]
 51‚ÜíComplementing this, a representation network works in tandem to refine and support the embedding spaces, ensuring robust joint representations without relying on generative reconstruction at the pixel level.
 52‚Üí[1]
 53‚ÜíThe self-supervised components of V-JEPA 2 emphasize non-generative prediction, allowing the model to learn predictive mappings in latent spaces derived from video data, which promotes efficient understanding of world evolution through embedding-based forecasting rather than explicit image synthesis.
 54‚Üí[1]
 55‚Üí[8]
 56‚ÜíTraining Methodology
 57‚ÜíV-JEPA 2 is trained using a two-stage self-supervised learning approach that leverages the joint embedding predictive architecture (JEPA) to predict latent representations of future video frames rather than raw pixels, which helps avoid the pitfalls of generative modeling such as blurriness and computational inefficiency.
 58‚Üí[3]
 59‚Üí[2]
 60‚ÜíIn the first stage, the model's encoder and predictor are pre-trained on internet-scale unlabeled video data to develop general world modeling capabilities, enabling the system to learn robust representations of visual dynamics without requiring explicit labels.
 61‚Üí[3]
 62‚Üí[2]
 63‚ÜíThis phase utilizes vast datasets comprising over a million hours of diverse video footage, equivalent to billions of frames, sourced from public internet videos to capture a broad spectrum of real-world scenarios.
 64‚Üí[4]
 65‚Üí[2]
 66‚ÜíThe second stage involves fine-tuning the pre-trained model with a small amount of interaction data, such as robot trajectories, to adapt it for specific applications like zero-shot control in robotics tasks.
 67‚Üí[2]
 68‚ÜíThis fine-tuning enhances the model's ability to predict action-conditioned future states, building on the foundational representations from the initial pre-training while minimizing the need for extensive labeled data.
 69‚Üí[1]
 70‚ÜíOptimization techniques, including scalable distributed training on large GPU clusters, ensure efficient handling of the model's 1.2 billion parameters and the massive data scale, allowing for rapid convergence and high-fidelity predictions.
 71‚Üí[4]
 72‚Üí[3]
 73‚ÜíOverall, this methodology emphasizes scalability and efficiency, drawing from self-supervised paradigms to advance world models that support downstream tasks in visual understanding and physical reasoning.
 74‚Üí[2]
 75‚ÜíCapabilities and Applications
 76‚ÜíVisual Prediction and Understanding
 77‚ÜíV-JEPA 2 achieves state-of-the-art performance in visual understanding tasks, such as action recognition and scene prediction, by leveraging self-supervised learning on large-scale video data.
 78‚Üí[1]
 79‚ÜíThis model excels in interpreting complex video sequences, enabling it to identify actions and anticipate scene evolutions without relying on labeled datasets.
 80‚Üí[2]
 81‚ÜíFor instance, it demonstrates superior accuracy in recognizing human activities and predicting environmental changes in dynamic videos, surpassing previous benchmarks in these areas.
 82‚Üí[3]
 83‚ÜíAt its core, V-JEPA 2 employs a mechanism for predicting future latent states in videos, which allows it to model world dynamics through non-generative forecasting without explicit supervision.
 84‚Üí[1]
 85‚ÜíThis approach involves encoding video frames into a joint embedding space and using predictive architectures to forecast subsequent states, capturing temporal dependencies and physical consistencies inherent in the data.
 86‚Üí[2]
 87‚ÜíBy focusing on latent representations rather than pixel-level reconstruction, the model efficiently learns robust representations of visual dynamics, building on the foundational Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) framework.
 88‚Üí[3]
 89‚ÜíA key strength of V-JEPA 2 lies in its zero-shot generalization to unseen video environments for basic prediction tasks, where it can forecast outcomes in novel settings without additional training.
 90‚Üí[1]
 91‚ÜíFor example, the model successfully predicts object trajectories and scene transitions in unfamiliar real-world videos, such as those involving everyday interactions or natural movements, by transferring learned world models to new domains.
 92‚Üí[2]
 93‚ÜíThis capability highlights its potential for broad visual understanding, as it adapts predictions across diverse video sources while maintaining high fidelity to underlying dynamics.
 94‚Üí[3]
 95‚ÜíRobotics Control
 96‚ÜíV-JEPA 2 enables zero-shot robot control by leveraging its video prediction capabilities to guide actions in novel environments without requiring task-specific training data. The model, when integrated with robotic hardware such as Franka Emika Panda arms, uses predicted future video frames to plan and execute manipulation tasks, allowing robots to interact with unfamiliar objects autonomously. This approach relies on the model's self-supervised learning from large-scale video datasets to infer physical dynamics and generate action sequences that align with desired image goals.
 97‚Üí[1]
 98‚ÜíIn practical deployments, V-JEPA 2 has been tested on real-world robotic setups across different laboratories, demonstrating its ability to perform tasks like grasping and pick-and-place operations. For instance, post-training on a modest amount of robot interaction video‚Äîless than 62 hours‚Äîenables the model to achieve high success rates in zero-shot scenarios, such as picking up and placing objects using visual predictions without explicit reward signals or labels. This integration highlights V-JEPA 2's potential for scalable robotics applications, where the model's world modeling supports direct control execution in unseen settings.
 99‚Üí[9]
100‚Üí[3]
101‚ÜíBenchmarks from Meta's evaluations show that V-JEPA 2-AC, a variant fine-tuned for action-conditioned predictions, attains success rates of 65‚Äì80% on manipulation tasks including grasping, reaching with objects, and placing in diverse lab environments. These results underscore the model's efficacy in translating video-based predictions into precise robot commands, facilitating zero-shot planning that adapts to variations in object shapes, positions, and lighting conditions. By focusing on predictive planning, V-JEPA 2 bridges the gap between simulated world understanding and physical robot control, paving the way for more generalizable AI-driven robotics.
102‚Üí[1]
103‚Üí[10]
104‚ÜíPhysical Reasoning and Planning
105‚ÜíV-JEPA 2 shows promising physical reasoning capabilities by predicting the evolution of the physical world from video data, enabling models to infer object dynamics and causal interactions without explicit supervision.
106‚Üí[1]
107‚ÜíThis capability stems from its self-supervised training on large-scale video datasets, where the model learns to represent and anticipate how objects move, collide, and interact in three-dimensional space. For instance, it can forecast trajectories of falling objects or the outcomes of physical manipulations, capturing underlying physical interactions through latent representations rather than pixel-level reconstruction.
108‚Üí[1]
109‚ÜíIn supporting planning tasks, V-JEPA 2 simulates future states of dynamic environments to facilitate decision-making in AI agents, allowing for the evaluation of hypothetical actions in simulated scenarios. By generating plausible future video frames or embeddings, the model aids in path planning or sequence optimization, where agents can explore multiple potential outcomes to select optimal strategies. This simulation-based approach enhances efficiency in complex environments by reducing the need for real-world trial-and-error.
110‚Üí[1]
111‚ÜíThe architecture of V-JEPA 2 advances machine intelligence through scalable world models that infer physical laws directly from observational data, promoting generalization across diverse scenarios without hardcoded rules. These models scale with data and compute, which are foundational for more robust AI systems. Researchers at Meta have highlighted how this inference mechanism allows V-JEPA 2 to handle unseen physical phenomena, such as novel object interactions, by leveraging joint embedding predictions.
112‚Üí[1]
113‚ÜíEvaluation and Impact
114‚ÜíBenchmarks and Performance Metrics
115‚ÜíV-JEPA 2 was evaluated using several established benchmarks for video understanding and prediction, demonstrating state-of-the-art performance in key areas. On the Something-Something v2 dataset, which assesses motion understanding through video classification tasks, V-JEPA 2 achieved a top-1 accuracy of 77.3%. Similarly, on the Epic-Kitchens-100 dataset for human action anticipation, it attained a recall-at-5 score of 39.7, outperforming prior task-specific models.
116‚Üí[2]
117‚ÜíFor video question-answering tasks, an aligned version of V-JEPA 2, scaled to 8 billion parameters and integrated with a large language model, scored 84.0 on the PerceptionTest benchmark and 76.9 on the TempCompass benchmark. These results highlight its capabilities in zero-shot visual understanding, where the model interprets and reasons about video content without task-specific fine-tuning. Evaluation was conducted on custom video corpora, including over 22 million videos for training, emphasizing real-world manipulation and physical interactions.
118‚Üí[2]
119‚Üí[11]
120‚ÜíMeta introduced three new benchmarks specifically designed to evaluate physical reasoning in video-trained world models, addressing gaps in assessing nuanced interactions like object dynamics and causal inference from video data. These benchmarks, made openly available, measure performance on tasks such as predicting physical world evolution and reasoning about interventions, with human baselines achieving 85%‚Äì95% accuracy, revealing a significant gap for current AI models.
121‚Üí[1]
122‚Üí[12]
123‚ÜíIn robotics applications, the action-conditioned variant V-JEPA 2-AC, trained on less than 62 hours of unlabeled videos from the Droid dataset, enabled zero-shot control for tasks like picking and placing objects on Franka robotic arms in unseen environments, achieving success rates of 65%‚Äì80%. This performance was tested on custom datasets of robot manipulation videos, focusing on generalization without environment-specific data collection.
124‚Üí[2]
125‚ÜíComparisons to Prior Models
126‚ÜíV-JEPA 2 represents a significant advancement over the original Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) and its video-specific predecessor, V-JEPA, primarily through its expanded scale and enhanced predictive capabilities. While the original JEPA, introduced by Yann LeCun and colleagues in 2022, focused on non-generative predictive learning for images and audio using smaller models, V-JEPA 2 scales to 1.2 billion parameters, enabling more robust video understanding without pixel-level reconstruction.
127‚Üí[1]
128‚ÜíThis contrasts with V-JEPA, which used models up to 1 billion parameters.
129‚Üí[13]
130‚ÜíIn terms of zero-shot capabilities, V-JEPA 2 demonstrates effective transfer to robotics tasks without fine-tuning, such as achieving success rates of 65%‚Äì80% in pick-and-place tasks with new objects in unseen environments.
131‚Üí[1]
132‚ÜíThe original JEPA lacked video-specific extensions, limiting it to static predictions, whereas V-JEPA 2 supports prediction and planning in dynamic video environments. These gains stem from refined predictor-target alignments that reduce overfitting, allowing better generalization across diverse video domains.
133‚ÜíCompared to generative video models like Sora or VideoPoet, V-JEPA 2 emphasizes efficiency by predicting abstract representations rather than raw pixels. Unlike earlier self-supervised approaches such as Time-Contrastive Networks (TCN), which relied on contrastive losses and struggled with long-term dependencies, V-JEPA 2's predictive architecture highlights its edge in scalable world modeling without explicit supervision. This non-generative focus also mitigates issues like mode collapse seen in diffusion-based models, enabling more reliable planning in dynamic environments.
134‚ÜíBroader Implications for AI
135‚ÜíV-JEPA 2 represents a significant advancement in self-supervised learning paradigms, particularly by demonstrating how large-scale video data can train scalable AI agents capable of generalizing across diverse physical environments without extensive labeled datasets. By leveraging the Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) framework, it enables models to learn predictive representations from raw video inputs, fostering the development of autonomous systems that can adapt to real-world scenarios with minimal supervision.
136‚Üí[1]
137‚Üí[2]
138‚ÜíThe model's emphasis on world models that simulate physical understanding brings AI closer to human-like intelligence, allowing for intuitive prediction of object interactions and environmental dynamics, which could underpin more robust reasoning in complex tasks such as robotic planning. This approach not only enhances predictive capabilities but also paves the way for AI systems that internalize causal structures of the physical world, potentially accelerating progress toward general artificial intelligence.
139‚Üí[14]
140‚Üí[15]
141‚ÜíMeta's open-source release of V-JEPA 2 encourages widespread community experimentation and innovation in AI robotics, promoting collaborative advancements.

Abstract
A major challenge for modern AI is to learn to understand the world and learn to act largely by observation
(LeCun,
2022
This paper explores a self-supervised approach that combines internet-scale video data with a small amount of interaction data (robot trajectories), to develop models capable of understanding, predicting, and planning in the physical world. We first pre-train an action-free joint-embedding-predictive architecture, V-JEPA 2, on a video and image dataset comprising over 1 million hours of internet video. V-JEPA 2 achieves strong performance on motion understanding (77.3 top-1 accuracy on Something-Something v2) and state-of-the-art performance on human action anticipation (39.7 recall-at-5 on Epic-Kitchens-100) surpassing previous task-specific models. Additionally, after aligning V-JEPA 2 with a large language model, we demonstrate state-of-the-art performance on multiple video question-answering tasks at the 8 billion parameter scale (e.g., 84.0 on PerceptionTest, 76.9 on TempCompass). Finally, we show how self-supervised learning can be applied to robotic planning tasks by post-training a latent action-conditioned world model, V-JEPA 2-AC, using less than 62 hours of unlabeled robot videos from the Droid dataset. We deploy V-JEPA 2-AC zero-shot on Franka arms in two different labs and enable picking and placing of objects using planning with image goals. Notably, this is achieved without collecting any data from the robots in these environments, and without any task-specific training or reward. This work demonstrates how self-supervised learning from web-scale data and a small amount of robot interaction data can yield a world model capable of planning in the physical world.

 54‚ÜíIntroduction
 55‚ÜíHumans have the ability to adapt and generalize when taking on new tasks and operating in unfamiliar environments.
 56‚ÜíSeveral cognitive learning theories suggest that humans learn an internal model of the world by integrating low-level sensory inputs to represent and predict future states
 57‚Üí(Craik,
 58‚Üí1967
 59‚Üí; Rao and Ballard,
 60‚Üí1999
 61‚Üí, and they further posit that this world model shapes our perception at any given moment, playing a crucial role in informing our understanding of reality
 62‚Üí(Friston,
 63‚Üí2010
 64‚Üí; Clark,
 65‚Üí2013
 66‚Üí; Nortmann et al.,
 67‚Üí2015
 68‚ÜíMoreover, our ability to predict the effects of our actions on future states of the world is also essential for goal-oriented planning
 69‚Üí(Sutton and Barto,
 70‚Üí1981
 71‚Üí1998
 72‚Üí; Ha and Schmidhuber,
 73‚Üí2018
 74‚Üí; Wolpert and Ghahramani,
 75‚Üí2000
 76‚ÜíBuilding artificial agents that learn a world model from sensory data, such as video, could enable them to
 77‚Üíunderstand
 78‚Üíthe physical world,
 79‚Üípredict
 80‚Üífuture states, and effectively ‚Äî like humans ‚Äî
 81‚Üíplan
 82‚Üíin new situations, resulting in systems capable of tackling tasks that have not been encountered before.
 83‚ÜíFigure 1
 84‚ÜíV-JEPA 2 Overview.
 85‚ÜíLeveraging 1M hours of internet-scale video and 1M images, we pretrain the V-JEPA 2 video model using a visual mask denoising objective
 86‚Üí(Bardes et al.,
 87‚Üí2024
 88‚Üí; Assran et al.,
 89‚Üí2023
 90‚Üí, and leverage this model for downstream tasks such as action classification, object recognition, action anticipation, and Video Question Answering by aligning the model with an LLM backbone.
 91‚ÜíAfter pretraining, we can also freeze the video encoder and train a new action-conditioned predictor with a small amount of robot interaction data on top of the learned representations, and leverage this action-conditioned model, V-JEPA 2-AC, for downstream robot manipulation tasks using planning within a model predictive control loop.
 92‚ÜíPrevious works have explored the development of predictive world models from interaction data consisting of state-action sequences, often also relying on explicit reward feedback from the environment to infer goals
 93‚Üí(Sutton and Barto,
 94‚Üí1981
 95‚Üí; Fragkiadaki et al.,
 96‚Üí2015
 97‚Üí; Ha and Schmidhuber,
 98‚Üí2018
 99‚Üí; Hafner et al.,
100‚Üí2019b
101‚Üí; Hansen et al.,
102‚Üí2022
103‚Üí. However, the limited availability of real-world interaction data constrains the scalability of these methods.
104‚ÜíTo address this limitation, more recent works have leveraged both internet-scale video and interaction data towards training action-conditioned video generation models for robot control, but only demonstrate limited results in robot execution using model-based control
105‚Üí(Hu et al.,
106‚Üí2023
107‚Üí; Yang et al.,
108‚Üí2024b
109‚Üí; Bruce et al.,
110‚Üí2024
111‚Üí; Agarwal et al.,
112‚Üí2025
113‚ÜíIn particular, this line of research often emphasizes the evaluation of the faithfulness of the predictions and visual quality instead of planning capabilities, perhaps due to the computational cost of planning by generating video.
114‚ÜíIn this work, we build upon the self-supervised hypothesis as a means to learn world models that capture background knowledge of the world largely from observation. Specifically, we leverage the joint-embedding predictive architecture (JEPA)
115‚Üí(LeCun,
116‚Üí2022
117‚Üí, which learns by making predictions in a learned representation space.
118‚ÜíIn contrast to approaches that focus on learning entirely from interaction data, self-supervised learning enables us to make use of internet-scale video ‚Äî depicting sequences of states without direct observations of the actions ‚Äî
119‚Üíto learn to both represent video observations and learn a predictive model for world dynamics in this learned representation space.
120‚ÜíFurthermore, in contrast to approaches based on video generation, the JEPA approach focuses on learning representations for predictable aspects of a scene (e.g., the trajectory of an object in motion) while ignoring unpredictable details that generative objectives emphasize, since they make pixel-level predictions (e.g., the precise location of each blade of grass in a field, or each leaf on a tree).
121‚ÜíBy scaling JEPA pretraining, we demonstrate that it yields video representations with state-of-the-art understanding and prediction capabilities, and that such representations can be leveraged as a basis for action-conditioned predictive models and enable zero-shot planning.
122‚ÜíOur approach, V-JEPA 2, utilizes a stage-wise training procedure, beginning with action-free pre-training on internet-scale video, followed by post-training with a small amount of interaction data (see
123‚ÜíFigure
124‚Üí1
125‚ÜíIn the first stage, we use a mask-denoising feature prediction objective
126‚Üí(Assran et al.,
127‚Üí2023
128‚Üí; Bardes et al.,
129‚Üí2024
130‚Üí, where the model predicts masked segments of a video in a learned representation space.
131‚ÜíWe train the V-JEPA 2 encoder with up to 1 billion parameters and with more than 1 million hours of video.
132‚ÜíOur experiments confirm that scaling self-supervised video pretraining enhances the encoder‚Äôs ability to achieve visual understanding, including broad motion and appearance recognition capabilities, through probe-based evaluations and by aligning the encoder with a language model for video question-answering
133‚Üí(Krojer et al.,
134‚Üí2024
135‚Üí; PƒÉtrƒÉucean et al.,
136‚Üí2023
137‚Üí; Liu et al.,
138‚Üí2024c
139‚Üí; Cai et al.,
140‚Üí2024
141‚Üí; Shangguan et al.,
142‚Üí2024
143‚ÜíFollowing pretraining on internet-scale video, we train an action-conditioned world model, V-JEPA 2-AC, on a small set of interaction data using the representations learned in the first stage.
144‚ÜíOur action-conditioned world model is a 300M-parameter transformer network employing a block-causal attention mechanism, which autoregressively predicts the representation of the next video frame conditioned on an action and previous states.
145‚ÜíWith as little as 62 hours of unlabeled interaction data from the Droid dataset
146‚Üí(Khazatsky et al.,
147‚Üí2024
148‚Üí, we demonstrate the feasibility of training a latent world model that, given sub-goals, can be leveraged to plan actions on a Franka robot arm and perform prehensile manipulation tasks from a monocular RGB camera zero-shot in a new environment.
149‚ÜíTo summarize, we show that joint-embedding predictive architectures learning from videos can be used to build a world model that enables
150‚Üíunderstanding
151‚Üíthe physical world,
152‚Üípredicting
153‚Üífuture states, and effectively
154‚Üíplanning
155‚Üíin new situations; this is achieved by leveraging internet-scale video and a small amount of interaction data.
156‚ÜíSpecifically:
157‚ÜíUnderstanding ‚Äî Probe-based Classification:
158‚ÜíScaling self-supervised video pretraining results in video representations applicable to many tasks. V-JEPA 2 excels at encoding fine-grained motion information, achieving strong performance on tasks requiring motion understanding, such as Something-Something v2, with
159‚Üí77.3
160‚Üí77.3
161‚Üítop-1 accuracy using an attentive probe.
162‚ÜíUnderstanding ‚Äî Video Question-Answering:
163‚ÜíV-JEPA 2 encoder can be used to train a multi-modal large language model, to tackle video-question answering tasks. We observe state-of-the-art performance on 8B language model class on multiple benchmarks that require physical world understanding and temporal reasoning, such as MVP (
164‚Üí44.5
165‚Üí44.5
166‚Üípaired accuracy), PerceptionTest (
167‚Üí84.0
168‚Üí84.0
169‚Üítest set accuracy), TempCompass (
170‚Üí76.9
171‚Üí76.9
172‚Üímulti-choice accuracy), TemporalBench (
173‚Üí36.7
174‚Üí36.7
175‚Üímulti-binary short-QA accuracy) and TOMATO (
176‚Üí40.3
177‚Üí40.3
178‚Üíaccuracy). In particular, we show that a video encoder pre-trained
179‚Üíwithout
180‚Üílanguage supervision can be aligned with a language model and achieve state-of-the-art performance, contrary to conventional wisdom
181‚Üí(Yuan et al.,
182‚Üí2025
183‚Üí; Wang et al.,
184‚Üí2024b
185‚ÜíPrediction:
186‚ÜíLarge-scale self-supervised video pretraining enhances prediction capabilities. V-JEPA 2 achieves state-of-the-art performance on the Epic-Kitchens-100 human-action anticipation task using an attentive probe, with
187‚Üí39.7
188‚Üí39.7
189‚Üírecall-at-5, which is a
190‚Üí44
191‚Üí44
192‚Üí% relative improvement over the previous best model.
193‚ÜíPlanning:
194‚ÜíWe demonstrate that V-JEPA 2-AC, obtained by post-training V-JEPA 2 with only
195‚Üí62
196‚Üí62
197‚Üíhours of unlabeled robot manipulation data from the popular Droid dataset, can be deployed in new environments to solve prehensile manipulation tasks using planning with given subgoals.
198‚ÜíWithout training on any additional data from robots in our labs, and without any task-specific training or reward, the model successfully handles prehensile manipulation tasks, such as Grasp and Pick-and-Place with novel objects and in new environments.
